1.synthesis: God tells of two sisters, daughters of one mother, who played the whore in Egypt (vv.1–3): Oholah is Samaria (the northern kingdom), Oholibah is Jerusalem (v.4). Oholah “lusted after her lovers,” the Assyrians, and bestowed her favors on them and their idols (vv.5–8); so God “delivered her into the hands of her lovers,” the Assyrians who destroyed her (vv.9–10). The integrative thread is spiritual adultery punished by the very lovers it pursued: the allegory portrays Israel’s political-religious unfaithfulness as harlotry, and shows the bitter irony that the Assyrian “lovers” Samaria chased became the instruments of her destruction — sin betraying its devotees into the hands of the very thing they craved. Thus the chapter opens by establishing that the harlotry it will describe is not a private failing but a national, generational apostasy, whose pattern of pursuing foreign powers and their gods began in Egypt and persisted through every era of the kingdom’s life (v.8; Ezekiel 23:19).
1.historical_context: The allegory of Oholah (Samaria) and Oholibah (Jerusalem, v.4) develops the marriage-and-adultery metaphor for covenant unfaithfulness (Hosea 1–3; Jeremiah 3). “Lusting after” Assyria (v.5) refers to the northern kingdom’s alliances and adoption of foreign gods, for which it fell to Assyria in 722 BC — delivered to the very lovers it pursued (v.9).
1.literary_structure: This allegory (vv.1–10) parallels chapter 16, using the two-sisters figure to indict both kingdoms. Oholah’s fate (vv.9–10) serves as the warning example for Oholibah, whose greater corruption follows. The two-sisters allegory (vv.1–4) names its figures plainly — Oholah is Samaria, Oholibah is Jerusalem — so that the lurid imagery is never mistaken for mere story but read as covenant history under judgment.
1.theological_application: Spiritual adultery — trusting and worshiping anything in place of God — betrays its devotees into the hands of what they pursued (v.9; Jeremiah 2:19). The “lovers” we chase to our own ruin are sin’s cruel irony. The believer is warned that idolatry, whether ancient alliances or modern substitutes for God, ultimately enslaves and destroys (Romans 6:21), and is called to the exclusive faithfulness God’s covenant love deserves. The shared origin of the sisters “in Egypt” (v.3) traces the root of unfaithfulness to the very beginning of the nation, teaching that besetting sins often have deep roots that must be exposed and not merely managed (Ezekiel 20:8).
1.christology: The unfaithful sisters punished by their lovers (v.9) magnify the faithfulness of Christ the Bridegroom, who loved and gave himself for an adulterous people to cleanse and present them pure (Ephesians 5:25–27). Where Israel’s harlotry ended in destruction by her lovers, Christ redeems the unfaithful bride from her bondage and makes her chaste (2 Corinthians 11:2), the spiritual adultery of his people borne and forgiven at the cross.

11.synthesis: Jerusalem (Oholibah) “saw” her sister’s fate “yet she was more corrupt than she,” lusting after the Assyrians and then the Babylonians, sending for them and defiling herself (vv.11–16), until she grew sick of them and turned away in disgust (vv.17–18), all the while remembering “the lewdness of her youth” in Egypt (vv.19–21). The integrative thread is the greater guilt of Judah who refused to learn from her sister’s ruin: Jerusalem witnessed Samaria’s destruction for the same sins yet plunged into even deeper harlotry — a damning indictment that knowing another’s judgment without heeding it compounds guilt, the heart’s craving for idolatry growing insatiable rather than chastened.
11.historical_context: That Oholibah “saw” her sister’s fate yet became “more corrupt” (v.11) indicts Judah for failing to learn from the northern kingdom’s fall (Jeremiah 3:8–11). Her lust for Assyria and then Babylon (vv.12–16) reflects Judah’s shifting alliances and idolatries. The deliberately crude, shocking imagery (vv.19–21) conveys the repulsiveness of spiritual unfaithfulness.
11.literary_structure: This section (vv.11–21) escalates the allegory, Oholibah’s greater corruption (v.11) exceeding her sister’s. The shocking language (v.20) is intentional rhetorical force, exposing the ugliness of the sin it depicts. The escalation from Oholah to Oholibah (vv.11–21) is the structural engine of the chapter, each detail of the younger sister’s sin deliberately outdoing the elder’s to establish the greater guilt of Judah.
11.theological_application: To witness another’s judgment for sin and yet sin more is greater guilt, not less (v.11; Luke 12:47–48). The deliberately repulsive imagery teaches that spiritual unfaithfulness, however we dress it up, is in God’s eyes shameful and degrading. The believer is warned not to presume on the lessons of others’ falls but to take them as solemn warnings, and to see idolatry not as harmless preference but as the ugly betrayal of covenant love it truly is (1 Corinthians 10:6–11).
11.christology: Judah’s insatiable, escalating harlotry (v.11) exposes the incurable bent of the human heart toward idolatry, which no warning can cure apart from grace (Jeremiah 17:9). Only Christ gives the new heart that the prophets longed for (Ezekiel 36:26), cleansing the adulterous and creating in them a faithful love for God (Titus 3:5). The repulsiveness of sin magnifies the wonder of the Bridegroom who would die to purify so unfaithful a bride (Ephesians 5:26). Judah’s memory of “the lewdness of her youth” drawing her back to sin (v.19) shows the captivity of the unrenewed will, from which only the new birth in Christ delivers, exchanging the remembered cravings of Egypt for a new and holy desire (Romans 6:17–18).

22.synthesis: God turns Oholibah’s lovers against her: the Babylonians and their allies will come against her in fury, mutilate and strip her, and judge her “as women who commit adultery… are judged” (vv.22–30). She must drink her sister’s cup of horror and desolation, “the cup of your sister Samaria… a cup of horror and desolation” (vv.31–34), “because you have forgotten me and cast me behind your back” (v.35). The integrative thread is the cup of wrath drunk for forgetting God: Jerusalem must drink the same cup of judgment her sister drank, delivered to her former lovers, because at the root she “forgot” God and cast him behind her back — the cup-of-wrath imagery making vivid that her idolatry brings the full measure of deserved judgment.
22.historical_context: The judgment by her former lovers (vv.22–25) depicts Babylon’s siege and destruction of Jerusalem. “The cup of your sister” (vv.31–33) is the cup-of-wrath motif (Jeremiah 25:15; Psalm 75:8), the same judgment Samaria drank. The root cause, “you have forgotten me and cast me behind your back” (v.35), echoes Ezekiel 22:12.
22.literary_structure: This section (vv.22–35) pronounces judgment on Oholibah, the cup-of-wrath imagery (vv.31–34) being its dominant figure. The verdict “because you have forgotten me” (v.35) names the root sin, tying the chapter to the bloody-city indictment. The judgment oracle (vv.22–35) pivots on the cup imagery, moving from the agents of judgment (the former lovers) to the cup Jerusalem must drain, the two figures together conveying both the instrument and the measure of wrath.
22.theological_application: Forgetting God and casting him “behind your back” (v.35) is the root of all idolatry and brings the cup of wrath (v.33; Nehemiah 9:26). The cup-of-wrath imagery teaches that judgment is the deserved measure poured out for persistent sin. The believer is warned never to relegate God to the background of life, and is pointed to the only escape from the cup of wrath — the One who drank it in our place (Matthew 26:39). The verdict “because you have forgotten me and cast me behind your back” (v.35) locates the essence of sin not first in particular acts but in the displacement of God, warning the believer that all outward disobedience flows from this inward forsaking (Jeremiah 2:13).
22.christology: The “cup of horror and desolation” (v.33) that Jerusalem must drink is the cup of God’s wrath that Christ drank to the dregs in Gethsemane and on the cross — “let this cup pass… nevertheless, not as I will” (Matthew 26:39). He drank the cup of wrath for his people so they might drink instead the cup of blessing and the new covenant in his blood (1 Corinthians 10:16; Luke 22:20). The judgment for forgetting God is borne by the One who remembered us in our low estate (Luke 1:48).

36.synthesis: God charges both sisters together: they committed adultery with idols, even sacrificing their own children to them, and defiled God’s sanctuary and Sabbaths the same day (vv.36–39). Righteous men will judge them as adulteresses and murderers (v.45), and their lewdness will be brought to an end so that “all women may take warning” (v.48); “you shall know that I am the Lord GOD” (v.49). The integrative thread is the climactic indictment of idolatry that desecrates the holy and destroys the innocent, judged as a warning to all: the sisters’ sins reach their depth in child sacrifice and the brazen defiling of God’s sanctuary, and their punishment is meant to be a public warning that turns others from such lewdness to the knowledge of God.
36.historical_context: The combined indictment (vv.36–39) reaches its nadir in child sacrifice (v.37; cf. 2 Kings 16:3) and the simultaneous defiling of God’s sanctuary and Sabbath (vv.38–39). Judgment by “righteous men” (v.45) — the Babylonians as God’s instruments — applies the penalties for adultery and bloodshed. The purpose “that all women may take warning” (v.48) makes the judgment exemplary.
36.literary_structure: This closing section (vv.36–49) indicts both sisters together and pronounces their exemplary punishment, the recognition formula “you shall know that I am the Lord GOD” (v.49) sealing the chapter. The warning purpose (v.48) frames the judgment as instructive for all. The concluding indictment (vv.36–49) joins the two sisters in a single sentence, its recital of child sacrifice and sanctuary-defilement (vv.37–39) forming the moral nadir before the recognition formula closes the chapter.
36.theological_application: Idolatry reaches its depths when it desecrates the holy and destroys the innocent — even sacrificing children (vv.37–39). God’s judgment on such sin is meant as a public warning to turn others from it (v.48; 1 Corinthians 10:11). The believer learns the seriousness with which God regards the defiling of holy things and the shedding of innocent blood, and takes the recorded judgments of Scripture as warnings to flee idolatry and pursue the knowledge of God (v.49). The exemplary aim of the judgment, “that all women may take warning and not commit lewdness” (v.48), shows that God’s recorded judgments are pedagogical, written for our instruction so that we might not desire evil as they did (1 Corinthians 10:6).
36.christology: The child sacrifice and desecration that mark idolatry’s depths (v.37) stand in utter contrast to the Father who gave his own Son, not to a demon-idol but as the saving sacrifice (John 3:16), and to Christ who cleanses rather than defiles the sanctuary (John 2:16). The judgment that serves as warning “that all may take warning” (v.48) points to the gospel call to flee idolatry to the One who bore its judgment, that all might come to “know that I am the Lord” (v.49; John 17:3).
